sentences.text: Yes.
Well, during the week, we had our meals sometimes the children with the nanny or with the lady that helped us in the house.
Or we had somebody that was coming to clean the house, or you know, people that I think was important for my mother for us to see that they are people.
They just happened to be in a situation where they had to earn their living this way.
But Friday night, we were sitting around a table always, all of us.
And we always had somebody from out of town who was a visitor to this town.
And we had very often a widow, a lady that didn't have anyone.
I think her name was Sonja.
But the second name, I don't really-- and so it was conducted with great detail and with reverence.
And I mean, we just loved it, you know?
So whatever happened during the week, if we happened to have-- because the breakfast we had together.

But the dinners, if we happen to have together or for whatever reason we had to go-- my father had a business conference or whatever, but Friday night and Saturday lunch we always-- and dinner, as a matter of fact, whatever-- we always.
Because on Sunday, the business was open.
But Friday night and Shabbat was closed.
Gefilte fish, chicken soup, anything think you describe, we ate.
We were not really so keen to eat.
We were not very food lovers, really.
I think we needed to be coaxed.
But I think this is where I sort of believe I developed, we developed a love for sitting around the table.
And nothing I love more than when I have my friends around the table and we share a meal together, and we talk, and we reminisce, or we just exchange all kinds of tidbits, you know?
And I think this is where-- somehow it was we had all the time in the world, all the time.
There was nobody rushing you.
You could sit for three hours or for two hours.
And every Saturday morning, my father explained to me that it was a privilege for him.
He brought me always a bit of [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is?
[NON-ENGLISH] is like a sponge cake-- and little bit of wine in the morning and a little bit of [NON-ENGLISH], and that's the first thing he brought me every Saturday morning, yes.
Well, sometimes they were people who were orthodox.
And seeing that-- I didn't realize this, but perhaps they were not sure if the other people were really 100% kosher.
I thought really that everybody was.
But it could have been, because it was Upper Silesia, and the kosher meat would have been very expensive.
So it could have been that occasionally, maybe there was one, you know, that could-- that somehow was not 100%, could have been 99%.
But it was not good enough.
So if there were visitors, certain visitors for whatever-- to a function in shul, or, you know, let's say even if the rabbi had too many visitors.
Because the rabbi sometimes had other people, other rabbis visiting.
So I think that they sent them to us.
And I think we loved it, you know?
We were sort of-- we knew that this is what is.
What business was your father in?
My father was a timber merchant, so--
Well, all I know that the company was under his name.
And just like my grandfather was, IM Siegreich, so, you know, he would have been J Siegreich.
He had another timber mill in [NON-ENGLISH], which was-- not [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], which would be Chorzow.
Chorzow, C-H-O-R-Z-- something maybe a W-O-- no, the end, a W-- Chorzow.
You know O with a W?
I was there only once when I was very little.
And he didn't like going in the afternoons there.
Well, he had-- there were forests not far from Russia that I don't know all they belonged to him, or-- not that they belonged to him.
But I know that the trees were felled down.
And it was, you know, from the beginning, the trees, the trunks were brought in by train, or by ship, or whatever.
And then they were all cut down.
And there was not just wood, but there was building materials, all building materials.
And so it was there.
And it was in the other town, in Chorzow.
But that was really on my mother's side, that wanted my mother.
You know, that's how it was then that, you know, when a girl got married, she always, you know.
So my grandmother bestowed this upon.
And my father didn't need it and didn't want it.
And it was always like a sore in his back, so-- yes, I remember him talking about it.
Do you know how many people your father employed in his business?
Oh, at least 30.
As a matter of fact, they were not.
There were only a couple of them that were Jewish.
But I think that he believed that if somebody was a good worker-- and I don't think Jews really wanted to do this kind of work.
It was heavy.
It was, you know, heavy, physical work with the machinery.
But what I think it was as well, those people-- there were no unions then.
And if they didn't work, they didn't pay.
So what I remember, somehow, I thought again that everybody was like this-- that when they couldn't come to work for whatever reason, my father sometimes went in.
He took his tie off.
He was a very particular, very elegant man.
Today I don't think you would have difficulty in finding a man like this who really was so fashion conscious.
And so he went into-- he stepped in.
And then he told me, from since I was very little, that if this man is not working, he has got a family.
He hasn't got what to eat.
So to take food over but to make sure that I don't-- that I make sure that he retains his dignity, that if I offer him, I say that we are sharing, that my mother bought too much or something.
He left it to me to find a story.
So in the beginning, I was-- I was very scared, you know?
But then he waited downstairs for me.
Then I sort of got used to it.
That I think he told me about this probably a couple of times.
But what I could see that he believed, he had the touch.
I mean, whatever he touched turned into gold.
My grandmother was like this.
His mother was like this.
And my cousin Israel was.
I am not like that.
But he believed that because it was a gift, and he believed that a gift is given to you, it's very precious, any gift.
And everybody's has got a-- how he explained to me, everybody has got a gift.
It is just if you develop the potential for it, you know?
So I believe that he was a very elegant man, but I think had a lot of-- the way he looked, it was the one thing.
But he had a lot of-- he was very humble in matters like this, had a lot of humanity.
How big was the Jewish community in Siemianowice?
Well, there were 150 Jewish families.
Close to about 40,000.
So we really a very-- we were really very small in number.
But somehow, we were very visible.
Somehow, you know, they always sort of could smell out who was a Jew.
A very large business.
I think that most of them were well off.
This was one of the reasons why they went to live there.
You see, because further down, not far from there, let's say, a 3/4 of an hour's drive, you had a town that would be predominantly Jewish.
But there to establish yourself would have been very difficult.
So that's why my father said to me when I was in beginning of the fifth grade, he said, I am going to towards the end of the year, I am going to look for a buyer, for the right buyer.
Because I think we better shift to Krakow.
He wanted to sell and to shift to Krakow.
Why?
Because he felt that us-- the other children will be entering school.
The younger one was already.
She would have been in the first grade.
I don't remember any this-- her going to school.
But I think she would have been already.
And so I think he felt that we would have had a better chance.
Or somehow he felt that we took enough, that enough is enough.
Because it wasn't getting any easier.
You see, it was getting with the Germans becoming more powerful, it was getting really harder.
And perhaps something in business, although he was a-- I'd say he was a powerful man.
He was a very power-- and he had this kind of a presence where he entered the room-- both of my parents had when they entered the room, you know, everything stopped.
Well, I think that the adults would have behaved in a more subtle way than the children did.
But I think that, let's say, possibly, my father, because he was so engaged in the business, so he dealt with a lot of people really.
He wasn't really selling, you know?
Other people were selling the materials for him.
He, let's say, he had buyers from other towns.
And my mother was assisting him in this.
So they had meetings, you know.
So he probably was not so much-- he didn't see that much of it.
But I think that he must have occasionally felt it as well.
And I think that it irked him.
I think--
Yes.
So I think, you know-- put it this way, you could not escape the feeling if it was an atmosphere.
You know, no matter how successful he was, it was not really his territory.
So he must have-- if I felt it, he must have felt it too that it was not really his territory.
Because I think that he hoped by moving that there will be more Jewish people.
And they will be-- this, because it was a competitive, really small town.
It was not a large town, this.
So he was very well known.
But I think that that was one of the reasons.
But I think about everything else, he could have coped with it.
He was an adult.
But I think that he felt that it's not fair to his younger children for them to be in this situation.
And then you didn't think if you become stronger because of this.
Although I feel that in the long run, I've got certain resilience.
At times, have more resilience than other people who grew up in towns that were predominantly Jewish, when they had this-- when they were cushioned from all of this, you see?

Where they sort of, you know-- wherever they went, the non-Jews were there who looked after, who did the physical work, who did the cleaning, who did looking to after the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you know.
But there was a lot of poverty.
In a town like this where it was predominantly Jewish, it was a lot and lot of poverty.
And that was a time where the Jewish children, where they could not afford to go to high school.
Or if you-- I don't know one in how many thousands attended university, you did not sit.
You had to stand in the back.
Well, I mean, I felt it even before I started school, but not to that extent than when I started school.
It was always.
I mean, in this particular town-- I can only speak about this particular town in Upper Silesia, close to the German border.
I don't know exactly before Hitler came to power.
Because for as long as I can remember, Hitler was in power.
So I can't really-- there are not enough words to describe.
It becomes a part of you, you know?
Well, I think, again, the words became-- you heard-- I think there was more-- there was no warmth between the Jewish and the non-Jewish children.
There was no-- they were not friends.
Perhaps at times, even as strange as it might seem, perhaps they felt a little bit sorry for us.
Perhaps you were older.
Perhaps we were older.
Something else is when you are six and when you are 11.
But I think on one hand, it was worse.
In what way, because perhaps you understood more.
Perhaps, you-- exactly, you were restricted in your movements and the sheer brutality of it.
I absolutely did.
It doesn't mean that I was-- that it because I was hit.
But, you know, stones were thrown after me, snide remarks.
They felt-- I'm generalizing-- but they felt that they can do anything, and they could get away with it.
And they could-- they tested you.
And they tested how far they could go.
And your mentality, or our, I really mean our mentality, being on somebody else's territory was such that it was sink or swim.
Now you have to find a balance where you have to retain a-- I don't know how it sounds, but I think that we felt that our dignity, our inner dignity was very important.
It was pointed out to us that your human dignity at all times is important to retain it.
Whereas on the other hand, you had to be careful.
Because, you see, instead of responding, you could only respond in a certain way, not to antagonize them further.
Because you see, we were the weak ones on the surface, at least, at least, you know?
Because there was-- and some of them in Siemianowice, were very poor too.
You see, they were very, very poor.
The poverty was great.
I mean, again, I didn't see it.
But sometimes I saw it.
And I think so the ignorance was very great, very big.
And the education was poor.
Because, you know, the children had to go to work as soon as they possibly could.
And perhaps, very often they couldn't find work, not being able to-- not being very articulate, not being literate enough.
You mean immediate family?
Well, my sister Carmela died a few months before.
Really a freak incident-- I think she was running.
She fell.
She was with my sister.
And she fell.
That was not in her nature to run, somehow, but she did.
And she developed very quickly a blood clot in her knee, which went through the tummy to the heart.
And they took her to Krakow.
I can still see that-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can still see, when I was coming from school and my mother was waiting at the window and making certain signs.
I didn't know what she really means.
And she tried to tell me that my sister is sick, and she has to go with her to Krakow.
And they opened her up, but they couldn't do anything else.
So they came back to Bedzin.
And I don't know, maybe a day later-- it went just very fast.
They couldn't do anything.
That was Carmela.
Five.
So she's only one that has got really a grave, that was buried, you know?
Well, she was buried in Bedzin.
But that was not really exactly in Bedzin, close to Czeladz.
There is a town close to Czeladz where there is the cemetery.
Czeladz-- C-Z-E-L-A-D-Z.
Yes.
I was in Siemianowice.
Yes.
On the 1st of September, 1939, when it broke out, when I came from school, I was told that we are going to leave, and immediately, straight away.
And I said to my mother, seeing that before, I was sometimes standing where the trains are passing and the Jewish people are coming from Germany, passing through this town.
And they just had one bundle with them.
And I was always-- I had something over my arm and always was it flowers, or food, or a book, or whatever.
And I was always asking them about books, you know, if they have books?
What happened with their books?
And they said, just one book.
So I said to my mother, and what will-- Mom, how can I leave?
What will happen with my friends?
And she said, the same what will happen with you.
I said, no, I mean my books.
How can I go and leave my books behind?
She said, one book, just one book.
What book did you take?
I took-- would you believe it that I can't-- I took a book
that was in Latin.
And I asked my mother-- that was Sienkiewicz.
And I asked my mother when I was very little, what does quo vadis mean?
And she said, quo vadis domino, which means, interpreted, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means in English, what are you aiming at?
What are you trying to accomplish?
Or better, what are you trying to accomplish, human being?
So I thought, again, you know, whatever I needed, I went to my mother, you know.
And I knew that she knew how to answer.
So what happened next when you were told you were leaving?
Well, next, we were in a haphazard way, what I thought that it was a haphazard way.
But I think that my dad wanted to go to Russia.
And as he had friends, I mean, business acquaintances not far from there where-- this is where the trees were brought in, close to the border there.
I think I remember just that we were traveling with my grandfather.
My grandfather was with us.
And we were traveling-- I don't know exactly how to describe.
It was not a buggy.
But there were two horses.
And there was like a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], it's called.
You don't see it here.
And somehow, I was trying to think.
But it was like a-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] it was something like a buggy, but it was made out of wood.
And it was quite large.
And you put, you know, bits and pieces.
And we fit it there.
And I remember the planes flying over.
And I remember we were-- the bombs were falling.
And I remember, we were sort of automatically, as we went along, we were sort of jumping into the ditches on this side.
And somehow, mercifully, nothing happened, you know.
We were in one piece.
We heard all of those noises, the moaning, and groaning, and people being hit.
But I remember, I can see it when I talk about it, whatever I am telling you now, I can visualize it.
But the sounds escape me.
Mercifully, the sounds escape me.
Because I think the sounds were so horrendous, you know, that somehow I had to distance myself from something [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to visualize it, to see, actually.
It was so terrible, really, to see it.

So if this sounds somehow-- for self preservation, I don't know what it is in a human being's mind that you can sometimes lock out something or, you know, dispose of something and retain other parts of it.
Before we went over that Russian border, which was Brzesc-- something comes to mind-- Brzesc nad Bugiem.
Brzesc-- B-R-Z-- with those two-- E-S-C-- nad, which would be N-A-D, Bugiem, which would be B-U-G-E-M.
Yes.
And--
No.
It was a-- yes, it was a city.
But it was-- there was something like a stream, you know, like water.
That's what I remember.
But we were, on the way before that, we stayed-- I remember must have been Rosh Hashanah, must have been between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
Because I remember that they took something like a chook.
And you know, that was then-- that was a ritual that Yom Kippur, you know, they got around your head sort of for the chook, to have a good year, for you to have blessings.
And whatever was supposed to await you, the chook, that was like a-- they were like three times over your head.
And there was a blessing said.
So that must have been Yom Kippur.
Chicken, yes, yes.
And I remember the legs and all, you know, was over my head.
And I was petrified.
But as it was a dead chicken, you know?
But then I was very feverish.
I had something like a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], whatever.
So I remember even where the bed was positioned.
And when I was tossing and it was a huge bed.
It must have been a very elegant home.
So we must have been there between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, something like this.
And then I recovered enough for us to continue.
Whose home was this?
This was a home of one of-- it was empty, unoccupied-- one of my father's business acquaintances who could have run further and gave him the key.
I don't know, really.
I don't know those details.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, I think we observed it as all.
I think that whatever my parents-- I think they must have talked about it and made a decision that you take one day at the time.
So they made the best.
They still it was observed.
And whatever they could, it was observed.
So it never entered my mind that it's going to be otherwise.
Because this is the-- wherever they went, wherever my parents were, and my sister was, and my family was, even if it was for one day, that was my home.
So somehow, they were there.
That was your security.
And this was very, very strong.
And on top of it, if the rituals, and on top of it, if all of this was there, not, you know, I can't do it now, because I haven't got the time-- no.
It was there.
Time had to be found for this, regardless of the surroundings.
So that even then, I wasn't really scared.
I mean, I just was-- they are going to take care of me, I knew.
So and they took care me spirituality as well.
And I think I realized later, much, much later that it is that you are really-- no matter every day of your life is very precious.
But you have to live it as a whole person, not just in a physical sense.
So when you allow yourself for the other things to come true, then, you know, you feel it.
So this is what my, thanks to my parents-- they made sure that the atmosphere, regardless-- they could have-- I don't know what fear they felt.
They must have felt enormous fear.
It was so unknown what was in front of them, so unfamiliar.
But they made sure that we didn't feel it.
So I didn't feel fear in them.
So I didn't pick it up, you know?
Ruth, we're going to have to take a break now and change tapes.
